briefing.opening: Good morning. As the Special Spokesperson for Climate Change, I will now begin today's climate briefing on June 30, 2026.

sections.title: 🔴 Worsening Global Climate Crisis and Direct Impacts
sections.content: Key news reports released today warn that the global climate crisis is intensifying, with its impacts manifesting across daily life and natural ecosystems. Domestically, the worst-case scenarios for coffee production are being reviewed [1], and concerns have been raised that heatwaves are becoming a disaster beyond mere discomfort [2, 10]. Internationally, consecutive research findings have reported that historical heatwaves in Europe would have been virtually impossible without climate change [75, 81, 93, 98], with warnings that these could spread to the New England region [79]. Furthermore, it has been revealed that climate change is already affecting bird populations in the UK [78] and is a direct cause of the retreat of the Pine Island Glacier [83], threatening winter sports and the livelihoods of Puerto Rican fishermen [68, 85, 94]. Alongside government survey results indicating Israel's climate change preparedness remains inadequate [65], warnings have also emerged that climate change has become a women's health emergency and the 'current financial reality' for the pension sector [86, 87, 90, 91].

sections.title: 🌱 Accelerating Carbon Neutrality and Renewable Energy Transition
sections.content: Amidst the crisis, positive domestic and international movements towards carbon neutrality and renewable energy transition are actively being observed. The European Union (EU) has confirmed progress towards carbon neutrality by reducing greenhouse gas emissions by 17% over the past decade [19]. Domestically, efforts to expand renewable energy continue, with the unveiling of a 55GW offshore wind roadmap [5] and the Lee Won-taek transition committee announcing a plan to build 30GW of renewable energy over 15 years [52, 54]. Major corporations such as Hyundai Wia, Hyundai Rotem, Lotte Wellfood, Ottogi, and KT have published sustainability reports, setting carbon neutrality targets for 2050 [25, 27, 28, 34, 37]. Amorepacific, as the first private company to receive RE100 verification, increased its renewable energy usage by 40.5% [57]. Various local governments, including Yongin City, Eunpyeong-gu, Sokcho City, and Ongjin County, have been recognized as excellent local governments in the evaluation of climate crisis adaptation measures [7, 9], and are encouraging citizen participation by expanding 'zero food waste' campaigns and resource recycling education [17, 29, 30], and offering local gift certificate benefits for practicing carbon neutrality [24, 31].

sections.title: ⚠️ Dilemmas and Debates in Energy Policy and Transition
sections.content: In the process of energy transition to achieve carbon neutrality targets, complex dilemmas and debates persist. Domestically, a critical perspective has been raised, questioning the promotion of a hydrogen economy for carbon neutrality [15, 18]. Six economic organizations submitted a proposal urging the establishment of a rational greenhouse gas reduction pathway, emphasizing the realization of carbon neutrality [36, 38]. Alongside discussions on expanding renewable energy, some companies have advocated for nuclear power expansion [41], or suggested considering both nuclear power and LNG to complement the intermittency of renewable energy [50, 53]. Notably, a report indicating that China, despite record renewable energy generation, increased its coal usage, accounting for 62% of the world's increase in carbon emissions [47], clearly illustrates the complexity and challenges of energy transition. Internationally, the potential for geopolitical power restructuring due to climate change has been mentioned [3, 4], and ongoing struggles over the direction of energy policy continue, such as Sakhalin's attempt to end its decarbonization experiment [96] and the debate surrounding the development of the Rosebank oil field in the North Sea [99].

sections.title: 📰 Trends in Climate Change Research, Perception, and Governance
sections.content: Diverse movements are also observed in terms of public perception, research, and governance regarding climate change. New research has been conducted on how Americans perceive health harms from climate change [69], and a phenomenon where high-performing CEOs speak differently about climate change has also been reported [67]. Studies are also underway on the impacts of climate change on plastic packaging and inland transportation systems [74, 76, 84], and analyses of regional climate change patterns are active, with reports mentioning changes such as warmer winters and cooler summers in Minnesota [82]. Domestically, discussions are ongoing regarding Article 37 of the Framework Act on Carbon Neutrality and Green Growth [33], and an analysis has emerged that climate change is becoming a key factor in pension scheme participant outcomes [91]. Furthermore, climate issues are expanding into various sectors, with World Cup matches being discussed in relation to climate change [70, 97], and grassroots sports clubs undertaking projects to respond to local community climate impacts [95].

briefing.closing: Today's briefing has highlighted a complex situation where the severity of the climate crisis intersects with global efforts to overcome it. We must heed these warnings and further accelerate the momentum for positive change. This concludes today's climate briefing.

summary.phenomenon: The development of the Rosebank oilfield in the North Sea is being pursued.
summary.outlook: New research estimates that the Rosebank oilfield development would produce approximately 250 million tonnes of CO2, equivalent to the UK's emissions over 10 months. Labour MPs have urged prospective Prime Minister Andy Burnham to rule out this development.

summary.phenomenon: James Evans, a member of Reform UK, has been appointed as the chair of the Welsh Climate Change, Environment, and Sustainability Committee.
summary.outlook: A green think tank has warned that James Evans' appointment could undermine the efforts of ministerial scrutiny. Climate campaigners have questioned his appointment, citing his past criticism of green energy projects.
